name: Bezier

values.name: atX
values.type: Basics.Float -> Bezier.Spline -> { point : { x : Basics.Float, y : Basics.Float }, t : Basics.Float }

values.name: normalize
values.comment: Takes a bezier and normalizes it so that it goes from (0,0) to (1,1).

values.name: pointOn
values.comment: Given a spline and a proportion, return the point on the spline at that proportion.

values.name: splitAt
values.comment:
Split a spline at a particular parameter value, resulting in two smaller splines.

Note, this is _not_ the intuitive version you're thinking of.

values.name: splitList
values.type: Basics.Float -> List.List Bezier.Spline -> { before : List.List Bezier.Spline, after : List.List Bezier.Spline }

values.name: standard
values.comment:
A very common Bézier curve that goes from (0,0) to (1,1) with control points at (0.4,0) and (0.2,1).

This is the standard transition curve for a lot of animation systems.

values.name: toCatmullRom
values.comment: _Note_ This only approximates a Catmull-Rom spline, it won't successsfully round trip with `toCatmullRom` because the control points are lost.
values.type: Bezier.Spline -> { one : Bezier.Point, two : Bezier.Point, three : Bezier.Point, four : Bezier.Point }

values.name: toCss
values.comment:
Normalize and render to a string that can be used in CSS

e.g. cubic-bezier(0.1, 0.7, 1.0, 0.1)

values.name: toPath
values.comment:
Render a spline to a string that can be used in SVG

e.g. (M100,250 C100,100 400,100 400,250)

This uses absolute coordinates.

values.name: toPoints
values.type: Bezier.Spline -> { one : Bezier.Point, two : Bezier.Point, three : Bezier.Point, four : Bezier.Point }

values.name: trace
values.comment:
Given

  - `toPoint` function which takes 0-1 and returns a point, and
  - `steps` The number of steps to render

Return a list of splines that approximates the curve.
values.type: { toPoint : Basics.Float -> Bezier.Point, steps : Basics.Int } -> List.List Bezier.Spline

values.name: withVelocities
values.comment:
The math here comes the `fromEndpoints` constructor in elm-geometry

<https://github.com/ianmackenzie/elm-geometry/blob/3.9.0/src/CubicSpline2d.elm#L174>

name: Bezier.Spring

aliases.name: Parameters
aliases.comment:
The parameters that define a spring.

Check out the presets or `new` if you want some help constructing these values.
aliases.type: { stiffness : Basics.Float, damping : Basics.Float, mass : Basics.Float }

values.name: at
values.comment: Calculate the spring's current position and velocity given a spring, a duration, a target position, and an initial state.
values.type: { spring : Bezier.Spring.Parameters, target : Basics.Float, initial : { velocity : Basics.Float, position : Basics.Float } } -> Bezier.Spring.Duration -> { velocity : Basics.Float, position : Basics.Float }

values.name: new
values.comment:
Normally when working with springs you have to choose a stiffness and a damping and they'll have seemingly arbitrary values like 216 or 14. This can be very hard to develop an intuition for!

Your first option is to use one of the preselected springs.

The next option could be using this function.

It takes:

  - `wobble` (0-1) - How wobbly you want the spring to be.
  - `quickness` (0-1) - How quickly you want the spring to move.
  - `settleMax` (ms) - The maximum time you want the spring to take to settle.
values.type: { wobble : Basics.Float, quickness : Basics.Float, settleMax : Basics.Float } -> Bezier.Spring.Parameters

values.name: peaks
values.type: Bezier.Spring.Parameters -> Bezier.Spring.Duration -> Basics.Float -> { velocity : Basics.Float, position : Basics.Float } -> List.List Basics.Float

values.name: segments
values.comment:
Given a spring, a starting position and velocity, and a target position, calculate the list of Bezier segments that will approximate the spring motion.

This does assume that the spring settles. It will return a maximum of 10 segments.
values.type: Bezier.Spring.Parameters -> { position : Basics.Float, velocity : Basics.Float } -> Basics.Float -> List.List Bezier.Spline

values.name: settlesAt
values.comment:
We can detect when a spring will settle if it is underdamped (meaning it oscillates before resting)
    <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Settling_time>

    However for critcally and overdamped systems it gets a lot more complicated.
    Fortunately, I'm not sure that that's an issue as I don't think we want to model overdamped spring systems for animation.
    https://electronics.stackexchange.com/questions/296567/over-and-critically-damped-systems-settling-time

values.name: stepOver
values.comment:
Iteratively step through a spring to get the final position and velocity.

at is faster, but possibly less accurate?
values.type: { spring : Bezier.Spring.Parameters, target : Basics.Float, stepSize : Basics.Float, initial : { velocity : Basics.Float, position : Basics.Float } } -> Basics.Float -> { velocity : Basics.Float, position : Basics.Float }

values.name: zeroPoints
values.type: Bezier.Spring.Parameters -> Bezier.Spring.Duration -> Basics.Float -> { velocity : Basics.Float, position : Basics.Float } -> List.List Basics.Float
